response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm going to be honest with you because that's just how I am. Fake content on social media drives me absolutely crazy, and I see it all the time even though I'm only online a few times a week. People share the most ridiculous things without even thinking about it, and it stresses me out because you never know what's real anymore. When I'm scrolling through Facebook or Instagram after a long day of cleaning, the last thing I need is to see some fake news about my favorite football team or some made-up story that gets everyone worked up. It genuinely affects my mood, which is already not great most days. What really bothers me is how people just believe everything they see. I might not have gone far in school, but even I know you need to question things. My approach is pretty straightforward and maybe not everyone will like it, but it works for me. First, if something seems too crazy or perfect, I just ignore it. I don't have the energy to fact-check everything, and honestly, I'm raising my daughter alone and working full-time, so my time is limited. When I do see something that catches my attention, especially if it's about topics I care about like Christianity or local news here in Bari, I try to see if other people are talking about it or if it's just one random post. I've learned the hard way that sharing wrong information makes you look stupid, and I've been burned before. The thing that really helps me is limiting my time online anyway. Being on social media too much just makes my anxiety worse, so checking in only a few times weekly means I'm less exposed to all that garbage. When something really upsets me or seems suspicious, I'll ask people I know in person, like at work or after yoga class. Real conversations with real people help me figure out what's actually true. I'm not going to pretend I have all the answers or that I handle this perfectly. Sometimes the fake stuff still gets to me and ruins my day. But I've accepted that social media is just like that now, and the best thing I can do is protect my own peace of mind by not getting too involved in every controversy or viral post that comes along.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 22-year-old with high neuroticism (96) and low agreeableness (11), suggesting emotional reactivity and directness in communication. With high extraversion (75), she would express opinions openly but with underlying stress. Depression as a psychological feature means she likely experiences fatigue and negative affect regarding stressful topics. Limited education (primary school) suggests practical rather than analytical approaches. As a young widow with a child, working full-time as a cleaner, she has limited time and energy. Low openness (27) indicates preference for familiar, straightforward solutions rather than complex strategies. Her moderate social media use (few times weekly) means she's aware of the issue but not deeply engaged. Christian beliefs might make her value truth but also make her vulnerable to religiously-themed misinformation. The tone should be direct, somewhat blunt, emotionally expressive, and focused on practical coping rather than sophisticated media literacy strategies.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, emotionally expressive, stressed, practical, somewhat defensive
